title: TSR's The Classic Dungeon - RetroLook
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1fn9RPaSFvM

Daniel (@dungeondive) takes a fond, clear-eyed RetroLook at TSR's The Classic Dungeon — the 90s reissue of 1975's Dungeon!, the game that for many 40-somethings was their very first taste of dungeon crawling. He's blunt that it's about as basic as a board game gets, but its one standout asset is the board: big, spacious, the platonic ideal of a dungeon layout — he suspects it influenced Dungeon Crusade, which he's eagerly awaiting.

You pick a hero (each with a different attack colour, win-gold total and special knack), move room to room, and on entering an uncleared room draw a level-appropriate monster card; beat it on a 2d6 colour roll and claim treasure, which is almost purely points — like an old arcade game. Wizards draw a hand of spells (teleport, lightning, fireball) whose effects, frustratingly, live only in the rulebook. He shares house-rule fixes (roll initiative each turn; play to a lower point total) and notes a friend still plays it yearly with his sons. Not a great game — but an important one, a snapshot of how far the hobby has come.

----

Dungeon! was the gateway crawl for a whole generation, yet Daniel admits it's wafer-thin by modern standards. Is there an "important but basic" classic you still pull out for nostalgia's sake?
